executiveSummary.text:
Eutelsat has been absolutely destroyed — down ~51% from its 52-week high of €4.74
              to €2.33. The stock is sitting 47% above its 52-week low of €1.59, having recovered
              from the lows but still in a severe downtrend. This is not a value opportunity
              in the traditional sense — it is a business under structural pressure from multiple directions.
            
            
              The March 2026 contract terminations for Express AT1 and AT2 (Russian satelli

businessModel.text:
Eutelsat is a satellite operator — it owns and operates a fleet of geostationary
              communications satellites providing video, data, and internet connectivity services
              to broadcast customers, telecom operators, and enterprise clients globally.
            
            
              Revenue streams:
            
            
              Video & Broadcast (~40%): Satellite capacity for TV broadcasting
                — the legacy cash cow. Under pressure as viewers migrate to streaming.
              Government & Enterprise (~35%): Secure communications,
                military/government contracts, maritime and aviation connectivity.
              Mobile Connectivity (~15%): Backhaul for mobile networks,
                aviation and maritime connectivity.
              OneWeb LEO (~10% and growing): Low Earth Orbit constellation
                for broadband — the strategic bet. Operational but pre-commercial scale.
            
            
              Economic moat: orbital slots are scarce resources — once Eutelsat holds a
              geostationary slot, competitors cannot easily replicate it. Long-term contracts
              with broadcast clients provide revenue visibility. The moat is eroding as
              LEO constellations (Starlink, OneWeb) offer lower-latency alternatives.

financialSnapshot.text:
Note: precise FY2026 figures from LSE/Euronext filings should be confirmed. Revenue and EBITDA guidance from H1 2025-26 results available in the February 2026 half-year report filed with AMF. Confirm from company filings before trading.

  • Market cap: ~€1.1B (tiny — €2.33 × ~470M shares estimated)
  • Revenue: ~€1.2–1.4B (confirm from filings — declining from prior years)
  • EBITDA margin: ~65–70% historically (satellite operators have high opex leverage)
  • Debt: Significant — the €1.5B bond refinancing in March 2026 addresses near-term maturities but the debt load remains the central issue for this equity
  • Dividend: Likely suspended given financial position and debt burden
  • PE: Not meaningful — equity is in distress territory with restructuring talk

A €1.1B market cap on a satellite operator with €1.5B+ in debt is a sign of serious distress. The equity is being treated as a turnaround/restructuring story. That can work — or it can go to zero if the revenue decline accelerates faster than the debt can be reduced.

recentCatalysts.text:
March 2026: €1.5 billion senior bond refinancing — closes near-term
                liquidity risk. This was a necessary move but not a transformative one.
              March 2026: Contract terminations for Express AT1 and AT2 satellite
                capacity (Russian satellites, RSCC). Sanctions-related. A revenue headwind on top
                of an already declining top line.
              February 2026: €1 billion financing from French export credit agency
                BPI for OneWeb LEO constellation — validates the strategic investment and provides
                non-dilutive funding for the OneWeb buildout.
              February 2026: H1 FY2025-26 half-year report filed with AMF —
                key document to review for revenue trends and EBITDA trajectory
              2025-2026: Video broadcast revenue declining as European
                and global TV migration to streaming continues

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull (20%)
                OneWeb achieves commercial scale. Government contracts
                accelerate. Video business stabilises. Debt reduction plan credible. Satellite
                connectivity demand grows (IoT, maritime, defence). Stock re-rates to €4-6.
                Upside: €4-6.
              
              
                Base (45%)
                Gradual revenue decline. OneWeb neither fails
                nor flourishes in near term. Debt restructuring continues.
                Stock drifts sideways in €1.50-3.00 range. Upside: limited, downside: limited
                but real.
              
              
                Bear (35%)
                Video revenue accelerates decline.
                OneWeb burns cash faster than expected. Debt covenant breach.
                Equity raise at distressed price wipes out existing holders.
                Stock falls to €0.50-1.00. Downside: -55 to -75%.
              
            

            
              Expected value: (20% × €5.00) + (45% × €2.25) + (35% × €0.75) = €1.00 + €1.01 + €0.26
              = ~€2.27 base case. Essentially at the current price — the market is
              pricing in exactly this outcome. The bear case has a higher probability weight than
              the bull. This is not a compelling risk/reward at €2.33.

risks.risk:
Debt burden and refinancing risk (HIGH): The €1.5B refinancing
                addressed near-term maturities but the debt load remains enormous for a
                declining-revenue business. If EBITDA erodes faster than modelled, covenant
                breaches become a real possibility. This is the primary equity risk.

Revenue decline acceleration (HIGH): Video broadcast is
                structural decline, not cyclical. Every year, more viewers leave satellite
                for streaming. Eutelsat's addressable market is shrinking. The pace of
                decline will determine whether the debt trajectory is manageable.

OneWeb execution risk (MEDIUM–HIGH): OneWeb is the only
                meaningful growth lever. It is capital-intensive, pre-revenue at scale, and
                faces direct competition from Starlink. French export credit financing is
                helpful but does not guarantee commercial success.

Geopolitical risk (MEDIUM): The Express AT1/AT2 terminations
                (Russian sanctions) illustrate this. Eutelsat has exposure to markets with
                geopolitical tension. European government contracts partially offset this.

LEO constellation competition (MEDIUM): Starlink has
                dramatically more satellites deployed and a functioning commercial product.
                OneWeb is behind and needs to scale fast to be relevant in connectivity.

Liquidity / equity raise risk (MEDIUM): A €1.1B market cap
                and €1.5B+ debt means the equity is thin. If the business needs more capital,
                an equity raise at these levels would be massively dilutive to existing holders.

recommendation.text:
REDUCE. The stock is cheap on an absolute basis and the
              bounce from €1.59 to €2.33 is notable. But cheap is not the same as
              undervalued when the business trajectory is declining.

entryExit.text:
REDUCE. The stock is cheap on an absolute basis and the
              bounce from €1.59 to €2.33 is notable. But cheap is not the same as
              undervalued when the business trajectory is declining.
            
            
              Key levels:
            
            
              €1.75–2.00: Support zone. The 52w low at €1.59 is
                the hard floor — between there and €2.00 is where the stock found buyers
                in early 2026.
              €2.00–2.50: Current range. Hold only if already in.
                Not a compelling entry point.
              €3.00–3.20: First meaningful resistance — 200-day MA
                and prior support. Would require positive news flow to reach.
              €3.50–4.00: Overhead resistance from prior support.
                Break above €4.00 is the early signal of a genuine turnaround.
              €4.00+: Would require OneWeb commercial traction
                proof or a structural improvement in video revenue. Not the base case.
            
            
              Eutelsat is a watchlist name for me — not a buy. The OneWeb angle is
              genuinely interesting and worth tracking. The €1.5B bond refinancing removes
              the immediate bankruptcy risk. But the equity is not yet at a level where
              the risk/reward justifies a position. Monitor for OneWeb commercial contract
              announcements or evidence of stabilising video revenue.
            
          

          
            Conviction Trend
            Latest conviction: 41/100. Trend versus prior report: Flat.
